name: Bill Gates

people_also_viewed.name: Halla Tomasdottir
people_also_viewed.about:
I am the president of Iceland since August 1st 2024. Previously I served as the CEO and Chief Change Catalyst for The B Team, where global business and civil society leaders work together on confronting our crisis of conformity in leadership. I have long been on a quest to inspire and empower leaders to embrace purpose-driven, gender-balanced and principled leadership. I have passionately pursued this purpose as a business leader in Corporate America with M&M and Pepsi Cola, as the first female CEO of Iceland’s Chamber of commerce and as an executive and non-executive director for diverse businesses. An entrepreneur at heart, I joined the founding team of Reykjavik University where I founded the Executive Education department and led a successful initiative focused on empowering women and girls as entrepreneurs, leaders and investors. In 2007 I co-founded an investment firm with the vision to incorporate feminine values and ESG metrics into finance. The company made international headlines when it successfully survived Iceland’s infamous economic meltdown. In 2016, I ran for President as an independent candidate with no prior political experience and surprised everyone as I emerged from an initial 1% in the polls to becoming the runner-up with nearly 30% of the vote.

I have an international MBA from Thunderbird and have lived and worked in the US, the UK and across the Nordics. My work has led me to the TED stage several times and I have delivered keynotes and participated in dialogues about new visions for leadership for companies and conferences around the world. Newsweek named me to a list of 150 women who “shake the world” and the New Yorker called me a “Living Emoji of Sincerity”. Who needs a formal title when you can be an Emoji?

Twitter: @HallaTomas
Facebook: @hallatomas

activity.title: This is huge news for the fight against tuberculosis. The World Health Organization is recommending near point-of-care tests that can use tongue swabs to collect samples and diagnose TB within an hour—making testing faster, simpler, and more accessible.​ ​ Tools like this will reach more people, catch TB earlier, and start treatment sooner. Grateful to the scientists and partners who made this progress possible. https://b-gat.es/4clCCIx ...more
The breakthroughs happening in geothermal, fusion, and geologic hydrogen have the potential to help make clean electricity widely available to more people.

Reflecting on my time at the Breakthrough Energy Ventures Investor Summit last week, I’m left incredibly energized. The pace of progress is exceeding even my most optimistic expectations. It’s clear that real breakthroughs are happening, and they’re happening faster and more effectively than I imagined. What’s most exciting is the potential impact: These advances will help make reliable energy more accessible to more people around the world. That kind of momentum is inspiring to be part of. ...more

Well-lit rooms, reliable power, and cold storage for medicine are things many of us don’t think twice about—but in rural health facilities across Uganda, they’re far from guaranteed. Programs like this, which ensure access to reliable energy, can make a huge difference in people’s lives. https://b-gat.es/3PzhGFj ...more

There’s something powerful about a room full of people committed to building, deploying, and scaling the next generation of clean energy. At the Breakthrough Energy Ventures Investor Summit, I was reminded just how foundational affordable, reliable energy is to opportunity and prosperity. ...more

I expect that in a few decades, at least one of these technologies will be generating a significant share of the world’s power: ​ ​ Geothermal - New innovations are making it more accessible than ever before, with companies now drilling deeper and extending wells horizontally to unlock heat in more places. ​ Fusion - If successfully commercialized, it could be built almost anywhere, scaled up, and used to generate massive amounts of electricity. ​ Geologic hydrogen - One of the biggest energy surprises of the past decade. It's still in its earliest stage, but the potential is exciting.​ ​ The progress is real—and it’s opening up new paths to a clean energy future. ...more

When Breakthrough Energy launched more than a decade ago, energy innovation was scarce, even as demand for cleaner and more affordable options was growing. Today, breakthroughs are emerging across the entire system—and more people will thrive around the world as a result. ...more

I’m excited to be at the Breakthrough Energy Ventures Investors Summit this week. It’s one of the best places to see the future of energy, and I can’t wait to see how much progress has been made since the last time we came together: https://lnkd.in/gT66_N_b ...more

From rapid molecular diagnostics and AI-powered X-ray interpretation to shorter, all-oral treatment regimens, new technologies are transforming what’s possible in the fight against tuberculosis (TB) – the world’s deadliest infectious disease, which claims more than 1.2 million lives each year. With over 10 million people falling ill with TB each year, ensuring these breakthroughs reach the people who need them most is key. The Global Fund partnership is helping turn these breakthroughs into action scaling up new tools, bringing down costs and reaching the people most at risk. Read more: https://lnkd.in/eBjGBjew ...more ...more

activity.title: Congratulations on this exciting next chapter, Ankur! Your leadership and insight—especially across Africa and India—will play such an important role in shaping strategy. I’m excited to see the progress ahead.

When Dr Delors Mabout specialised in dentistry, he didn’t anticipate dedicating himself to the cause of vaccination. But when he moved to Cameroon’s conflict-hit Far North, he found the scale of need enormous. “We needed prevention, and vaccination was the way to go... Immunisation is an indispensable tool for protecting children.” -- Dr Mabout For this heroic health worker, vaccination must not be treated as a privilege, but every child’s right. Now he runs an organisation that brings together young health professionals dedicated to humanitarian healthcare and vaccines as a measure of equity in harsh times. Read more about his inspiring work: https://bit.ly/4uYEcYn

“As a person with a disability, I feel even more motivated to improve people’s health.” Meet Fatoumata Keita, the only laboratory technician at the Sébéninkoro community health center on the outskirts of Bamako – conducting malaria tests, helping ensure patients receive accurate diagnosis and timely treatment. Despite many challenges, she remains a critical first line of defense against disease. #WHWWeek Read more: https://lnkd.in/eWTS2a32
